meta.title: Pre-generated segment playbooks
meta.note: Plain text only. No API keys and no live model calls in the notebook. Edit this JSON to refresh copy, then re-run the Goal 5 cell.
meta.generatedFor: UCI Online Retail RFM segments (hackathon notebook)

Champions.description: Your best customers: very recent purchases, strong repeat activity, and high spend. They behave like fans of the assortment and keep the cash register moving.
Champions.action: Champions → double down on loyalty rewards, early access drops, and referral perks. Ask for reviews and UGC; protect margin with bundles instead of blanket discounts.

Loyal Customers.description: High frequency and solid monetary value with decent recency. They are dependable repeat buyers even if they are not quite in the top RFM corner.
Loyal Customers.action: Loyal Customers → nurture with subscription-style reminders, replenishment prompts for consumables, and tiered perks that reward consistency over one-off coupons.

Promising.description: Recent engagement with meaningful spend but frequency still has room to grow. They look like customers on an upswing if you give them reasons to return.
Promising.action: Promising → cross-sell complementary SKUs, limited-time bundles, and educational content (how-to, styling, care guides) that justify a second purchase soon.

New.description: Very recent first or early purchases with low frequency so far. They are still forming habits and have not proven long-term loyalty yet.
New.action: New → onboarding series, satisfaction check-ins, and a clear second-purchase incentive tied to categories they already bought. Keep service fast to cement trust.

Needs Attention.description: Middle-of-the-road RFM scores: not cold, not hot. They are easy to ignore but often respond to a nudge with the right offer.
Needs Attention.action: Needs Attention → light personalization (category affinity), small experiments on subject lines and send time, and surveys to learn blockers like shipping or assortment gaps.

At Risk.description: Historically engaged (frequency and spend were healthy) but recency has slipped. They look like drift, not beginners.
At Risk.action: At Risk → win-back campaign with a sincere "we miss you" tone, replenishment reminders, winback codes with a deadline, and optional human outreach for high monetary tiers.

Cannot Lose Them.description: Big spenders whose recency has gone stale. Losing them would hurt revenue disproportionately even if counts are small.
Cannot Lose Them.action: Cannot Lose Them → white-glove save offers, proactive service recovery, VIP concierge, and inventory alerts on favorites. Avoid spam; prioritize relevance and respect.

Hibernating.description: Quiet lately with modest past engagement signals. They are not the worst churn profile but they are fading from active memory of the brand.
Hibernating.action: Hibernating → low-frequency reactivation cadence, seasonal lookbooks, and "what is new since you left" highlights. Sunset to cheaper channels if no response after a few tries.

Lost.description: Weak recency, frequency, and monetary pattern. They behave like one-time or long-lapsed shoppers with little recent signal to build on.
Lost.action: Lost → suppress expensive paid reactivation, run rare high-value tests only, and focus acquisition learnings on why they never graduated to repeat buyers.

faq.q: Why is there no live AI call in this notebook?
faq.a: The hackathon layer is intentionally offline: insights are authored once as JSON so the dashboard stays fast, reproducible, and free of API keys in the UI.

faq.q: How should I refresh the copy?
faq.a: Edit ai_insights.json (or regenerate it elsewhere), save the file beside hackathon-notebook.py, then re-run the Goal 5 insight cell in marimo.

faq.q: How does "revenue per segment" relate to RFM monetary?
faq.a: Monetary here is the per-customer sum of line revenue in the cleaned data; segment revenue is that customer-level monetary summed for everyone in the segment.
